operations.rationale: Replace the autonomy and treasury mode tables with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research mode matrices.
content_lines: A steward should not start life as an autonomous project manager. The lowest mode is manual: it drafts plans, labels, summaries, and proposed contracts while humans approve every action. Assisted mode lets it perform low-risk hygiene and local validation, but protected branches, funds, releases, and governance remain human-gated. Bounded autonomy is narrower than the name sounds: ordinary work may proceed only inside recorded budgets, allowlists, logs, rollback paths, and appeal channels.

Community-governed mode changes the approval surface rather than granting sovereignty. Major changes route through maintainers, contributors, holders, guardians, delays, quorum rules, fork paths, and exit rights. Sunset mode is stricter still: the steward preserves, archives, transfers, or freezes the artifact while ordinary feature work stops until a sunset review changes the state.

Treasury behavior needs the same staging. Manual treasury means every spend is proposed and externally approved. Budgeted autonomy allows small repeated work below strict caps, with spend policy doing the authorizing rather than the steward. Bounty escrow reserves funds for verified work contracts. Recurring operations cover hosting, domains, CI, storage, and monitoring. Compute rental can pay for GPUs, runners, or cloud jobs only when data class and sandbox rules permit the exposure.

A governed treasury may use proposals and votes for larger changes, but legitimacy, legal authority, and economic control remain separate problems. Emergency freeze exists so continuity can yield to protection. In every mode, the steward can be a clerk for the treasury, not the treasury's sovereign.

operations.rationale: Turn the project-object catalog table into prose for the relaxed reader path without removing the canonical record vocabulary.
content_lines: The steward's records are deliberately plain because they need to survive across tools. The `ArtifactStewardCharter` binds the steward to one artifact and records mission, non-goals, maintainers, governance model, authority ceiling, budget policy, evidence policy, release policy, federation policy, and sunset criteria. The `ProjectWorkContract` turns roadmap intent into bounded work by naming the objective, context references, allowed files or systems, allowed tools, forbidden tools, outputs, acceptance tests, review gates, evidence references, budget, deadline, rollback, and non-claims.

The `ContributionLedgerEntry` records contribution without collapsing credit, money, votes, and evidence. Authorship, review role, evidence role, compensation reference, reputation signal, governance effect, and conflict notes remain separate. The `TreasuryPolicyRecord` bounds funds, compute credits, bounties, recurring operations, and freezes through policy id, artifact id, mode, autonomous limits, single-action limits, compute or rental limits, approval thresholds, protected spend classes, freeze authorities, and audit requirements.

The `EventTaintRecord` keeps repository and worker events from becoming privileged control text. It records event source, actor, surface, taint state, trusted fields, untrusted fields, forbidden control uses, review requirements, sanitized artifacts, and residuals. The `StewardActionDecision` explains what the steward proposed, ran, blocked, or escalated, including authority basis, inputs, rejected options, approvals, evidence references, residuals, affected artifacts, and non-claims.

The `SunsetReviewRecord` is the death-protocol object. It records value signal, active users, active maintainers, open risks, funds, dependencies, archival plan, fork or transfer path, and appeal path. Together these records make stewardship inspectable before it becomes machinery.

section.title: Beyond the State of the Art

operations.rationale: Replace the dense steward-OS target architecture with reader prose while preserving the canonical AI/research lifecycle-control checklist.
content_lines: A mature stewardship layer would be a project-lifecycle operating system, but not a project owner. It would keep mission, memory, roadmap, work, funds, compute, contributors, evidence, releases, governance, and sunset criteria inspectable across many human and AI work cycles.

The steward's power would come only from explicit records: a charter, work contracts, contribution ledgers, treasury policy, event-taint rules, release evidence, federation contracts, action decisions, and sunset reviews. Humans, maintainers, communities, project hives, personal hives, CI runners, and rented compute would receive scoped contracts rather than inheriting project authority.

The mature system would make spending, compute, contribution credit, governance influence, releases, appeals, forks, and exit paths visible without forcing people to ask the steward for permission to understand the project. It would also know when to stop: if value, maintainership, funds, mission coherence, or safety posture falls below the charter threshold, ordinary work generation should yield to transfer, archive, freeze, or sunset.

That endpoint remains unimplemented here. It should stay at `argument` support until workflow runners, treasury boundaries, event-taint pipelines, governance gates, release checks, sunset protocols, and behavioral tests show that stewardship can coordinate a project without seizing it.

operations.rationale: Replace the dense schema-and-probe inventory with reader prose while preserving the canonical steward record fixtures, lifecycle probe, and no-promotion boundary.
content_lines: The smallest honest steward is repository-local and proposal-first. It begins with a charter that names mission, non-goals, maintainers, authority ceiling, evidence policy, budget policy, allowed work, forbidden work, and sunset criteria. Work then enters through proposed contracts, not free-form agent initiative: each task should name allowed files or systems, allowed tools, required outputs, review gates, rollback path, and non-claims.

In that minimal form, the steward may label issues, draft plans, summarize pull requests, request CI, prepare decision logs, and propose work. It should not merge protected branches, spend funds, change governance, publish releases, promote evidence states, or assign outside workers without human approval. The early win is legibility, not autonomy.

The repository currently carries the record-shape slice for this idea. Schemas and valid fixtures exist for steward charters, work contracts, contribution entries, treasury policy, event taint, action decisions, and sunset reviews. Those records show that the public contract can be represented consistently, but they do not execute a steward, connect to a wallet, scan workflow events, run a governance process, or prove project safety.

A bounded lifecycle probe adds one more useful check. It accepts clean release-review and sunset-review routes, and it rejects tainted intake without review, over-policy spending, contribution-to-governance laundering, unscoped federation, release without gate evidence, and ordinary work after sunset criteria are met. That is still a fixture-level result. It is useful because it keeps the failure boundaries explicit before anyone treats stewardship as an autonomous project manager.
